title: Gazpacho Andaluz
subtitle: Chilled Spanish tomato soup
excerpt: Gazpacho is a cold Spanish soup of blended raw tomatoes, cucumber, pepper, garlic, vinegar, and olive oil. It is refreshing, sharp, and smooth. It needs at least two hours of chilling before serving, and it keeps well in the fridge for two days.
sourceNotes: Gazpacho andaluz originates in Andalusia in southern Spain. Earlier versions were bread and garlic soups without tomatoes; the tomato came to Spain from the Americas and transformed the dish. The cold, blended version is now closely associated with Spanish summer cooking.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps in the fridge for 2 days; the flavour improves after the first day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead for the best flavour.

content.text: Gazpacho is a cold soup of raw blended tomatoes, cucumber, and pepper, sharpened with vinegar and enriched with olive oil. It is smooth, refreshing, and deeply flavoured. The tomatoes are everything here; use the ripest, most fragrant ones you can find.
Combine all the ingredients in the blender. Blend on high speed for at least 2 minutes until completely smooth.
For a very smooth gazpacho, pass it through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing with the back of a ladle to extract as much liquid as possible. For a slightly more textured version, serve as blended.
Taste and adjust the salt and vinegar. Transfer to a covered container and refrigerate for at least 2 hours. Serve cold in bowls or glasses with a drizzle of olive oil.

items.cause: Out-of-season tomatoes, or not enough salt and vinegar
items.fix: Taste again after chilling; the flavour changes as it cools. Add more vinegar first, then salt. If the tomatoes were bland, a pinch of sugar sometimes helps.

items.cause: Blender not powerful enough, or not blended long enough
items.fix: Blend for longer. A high-powered blender is ideal. Passing through a sieve will remove most remaining texture.

items.cause: Tomatoes not very juicy, or too much bread
items.fix: Add cold water a splash at a time and blend briefly. The consistency should be slightly thicker than a juice but pourable.

content.text: Gazpacho is the quintessential summer food of Andalusia. It was originally a peasant dish eaten during harvest to provide hydration and energy in the summer heat. Outside Spain it has become one of the best-known representations of Spanish cooking. Serve it very cold, in a bowl or a glass, with the best olive oil you have to drizzle at the end.
